post: At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe citizenship doesn't begin at 18. The 2026 MyImpact Challenge proves it. This year, hundreds of students from 49 states and Washington, D. C. identified needs in their communities and took action through service, entrepreneurship, and volunteerism. Book drives. Business mentorship programs. Advocacy for people with invisible disabilities. A youth-led effort to bring digital literacy to seniors that has now reached more than 2,000 people in-person and over 3 million online. These are students who didn't wait for someone else to solve the problem. They saw a need, applied civic virtues like responsibility, humility, and justice, and built something real. Check out the 2026 MyImpact Challenge National Fair to meet this year's winners, including the sisters whose project took home the $10,000 Grand Prize. 👉  https://bit.ly/4fagPUD Know a student helping out their community? They can submit to the the 2027 contest starting September 11, 2026.  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #StudentLeadership  #BillOfRightsInstitute

David Bobb, president of the Bill of Rights Institute, explains why respectful disagreement is an essential habit for civic education that's rarely in today's classrooms. He also offers his take for whether schools can recover it. 🏫 Read more:  https://edw.link/tk9

In 2025, BRI ran 315 professional development programs across 27 states and Washington, D. C., reaching over 8,000 teachers. The programs we're most proud of aren't the ones with the highest single-day turnout. They're the ones where we're still showing up years later. Our PD is built around the Founding documents  the Declaration, the Constitution, the Bill of Rights  and the civic principles that come up in every election, every courtroom, and every classroom debate. We design programming to compound: each session builds on the last, and each year gives teachers a deeper foundation to teach from. Fall planning happens in July. Constitution Day is September 17. What would your district's civics program look like with a PD partner for the next 3 years, not just the next 3 weeks? 🔗 Request a program:  https://bit.ly/4fZoZ46  #CivicsEducation  #ProfessionalDevelopment  #AmericanHistory  #K12Education  #ConstitutionDay

How does an ordinary young man become an extraordinary leader? That's the question at the heart of Young Washington, in theaters now, and it's the one director Jon Erwin digs into on the Going Big! podcast. His answer runs counter to how we usually teach the Founders: Washington wasn't forged in success. He was forged in failure. As Erwin puts it, success is where you cash in, but failure is where you learn. It's a compelling listen, and a preview of why this story belongs in classrooms. BRI has built family discussion guides and curriculum resources to help students dig into exactly these questions. Scan the code for the family guide. Full interview below 👇 Thank you to Daniels Fund for making these resources possible.  https://bit.ly/4buMJKj  #CivicsEducation  #Leadership  #AmericanHistory

Happy Independence Day 🇺🇸 250 years ago today, the Founders declared something that has never sustained itself automatically: Free and equal people can govern themselves in the pursuit of happiness. Every generation must learn it, question it, and choose whether to carry it forward. The next chapter of the American story is already being written in classrooms across the country. We're grateful for every teacher and supporter making that possible. Happy 4th of July from BRI!  #IndependenceDay  #America250  #CivicEducation  #July4th  #DeclarationOfIndependence

27 grievances. One question beneath all of them: what is government for? The 27th and final grievance of the Declaration of Independence, posted the day before the nation's 250th birthday, addresses 2 of the most historically complex threats of the Revolutionary era. And it does so in language that Dr. C. C. Borzilleri addresses directly, separating the offensive attitudes of the founding era from the principles that still repay examination. Both complaints resolve into the same question: what is government for, if not to protect the people within its territory? For colonists who believed the empire had abandoned that obligation, independence was, in Borzilleri's framing, the next logical step to formalize a breakdown that had already occurred. 27 grievances later, the answer is the Declaration of Independence. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Every generation needs stories that remind people what's possible when someone chooses to keep going after failing. Young Washington is about a young man who was ambitious, imperfect, and tested by failure, closer to something most people recognize than a distant founding myth. That's why Stand Together supported the film's production and national marketing campaign alongside Wonder Project, Angel, George Washington's Mount Vernon, and the Daniels Fund, led by Hanna Skandera Grady, and the Bill of Rights Institute, led by David Bobb who built the educational curriculum bringing the story into classrooms nationwide. See Young Washington in theaters everywhere starting July 3, and grab the interactive discussion guide from the Bill of Rights Institute:  https://lnkd.in/gzcH4kjd  #YoungWashington

Tomorrow, America turns 250. There will be fireworks, parades, and speeches honoring the Founders, the soldiers, the reformers. As there should be. But I want to thank a group that rarely makes the list. Somewhere right now, a civics or history teacher is planning a lesson on the Declaration of Independence. About 200,000 secondary civics and history teachers serve some 20 million students every year. Another 2 million elementary teachers give millions of kids their very first encounter with America's story. They are teaching not only what America is, but what it aspires to be. Frederick Douglass called the Declaration's ideals the "saving principles." But those principles survive only if they're passed onand it's teachers who are among the principal stewards of that inheritance. Thank you, teachers. The next 250 years depend on you. I wrote about why in the Atlanta Journal-Constitution. Link in the first comment.  #CivicsEducation  #Teachers  #America250

A high school history teacher told us something that's stayed with me. Her students have stopped arguing in class. They're afraid that if they say what they think, they'll be judged by the kids sitting next to them. Here's what I keep coming back to as we mark America's 250th: this country wasn't founded on the absence of disagreement, but the disciplined practice of it. That's what I explore in Divided Over the Declaration, my new book with Tony Williamsthe Declaration of Independence was born from contentious debate, and so were the 19th Amendment and the Civil Rights Act. A silent classroom isn't a peaceful one. It's a warning that we need to renew debate and civil discourse, in our classrooms and with our neighbors and fellow citizens. I wrote about what we can do to teach and model strong civics in Education Week. Link in the first comment.  #CivicsEducation  #Teachers  #America250

America is polarized. We're tired of debating. We can't seem to agree on anything. David Bobb and Tony Williams have a counterintuitive response: that's the point. In this BRI Scholar Talk, David Bobb and Tony Williams make the case that disagreement over the Declaration of Independence has been the engine of American progress. From freedom petitions by enslaved people in the early Republic, to Seneca Falls, to the March on Washington, the Declaration's ideals were never simply handed down. They were argued for, fought for, and claimed. Their book Divided Over the Declaration traces 250 years of that debate. This conversation is a compelling place to start. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4gzLCwz  #A250  #Declaration  #Progress
